prompt_md: A team maintains a `Storage` contract with two adapters: an in-process store (per-instance, never fails, no size cap) and a networked store (shared across instances, can time out, rejects values over 1 MB). They want to switch the default from the in-process adapter to the networked one. The callers were written years ago against the in-process behaviour and have **no error handling and no size checks**. What should the team do, and why?

decision_choices: Flip the config to the networked adapter immediately; identical signatures mean callers will behave the same
First make the contract's behavioural promises explicit (reads may fail, data is shared, values are capped) and update callers to honour them, then switch
Keep both adapters but never document the differences, leaving callers to discover them in production
Abandon the contract entirely and hard-code the networked store's SDK into every caller
reason_choices: Because matching signatures hide behavioural differences (failure modes, consistency, size limits), so the seam is only truly swappable once callers honour the same promises
Because a one-line config change can never cause a production incident
Because hiding differences keeps the contract looking clean and that is the main goal
Because coupling every caller directly to a vendor SDK is the recommended way to manage change
